event: Early hominins leave stone tools at Ain Boucherit, among the oldest evidence of human ancestors in North Africa.
Aterian culture and later prehistoric peoples occupy the Maghreb, leaving sophisticated stone tools across the region.
Saharan rock art at Tassili n'Ajjer begins, depicting a green Sahara with herds, hunters, and pastoral life.
Phoenician traders from Tyre establish coastal trading posts along the Algerian shoreline.
Carthage is founded nearby, drawing the Algerian coast into the Punic commercial and cultural orbit.
Numidian king Masinissa, allied with Rome, helps defeat Carthage at Zama and unites a powerful Berber kingdom.
Masinissa dies after building Numidia into a prosperous, urbanized Berber state spanning much of modern Algeria.
The Jugurthine War erupts as Numidian king Jugurtha resists Roman domination of North Africa.
Rome annexes Numidia following Julius Caesar's victory at Thapsus, ending its independence.
Juba II is installed as a Roman client king of Mauretania, fostering a Hellenistic-Berber court culture.
Emperor Caligula annexes Mauretania, and Roman provinces flourish with cities like Timgad, Djemila, and Tipasa.
Christianity spreads widely in Roman Africa, producing influential figures and the later theologian Augustine.
The Vandals invade from Spain, seizing the North African coast and weakening Roman control.
Byzantine general Belisarius reconquers North Africa, restoring imperial rule over coastal Algeria.
Arab Muslim armies begin their conquest of the Maghreb, initiating the gradual Islamization of the region.
Berber and Arab forces from the Maghreb cross into Iberia, launching the conquest of Visigothic Spain.
The Rustamid imamate is founded at Tahert, creating a prosperous Ibadi Berber state and trans-Saharan trade hub.
The Fatimids rise in the Maghreb before shifting their power base eastward toward Egypt.
The Berber Zirid and Hammadid dynasties govern central Maghreb, founding cities such as the Qal'a of the Beni Hammad.
The Almoravids extend their empire across the western Maghreb, promoting a reformist Islam.
The Almohad caliphate unifies much of North Africa and Iberia under Berber rule.
The Zayyanid dynasty establishes its kingdom at Tlemcen, a thriving center of trade and learning.
The corsair Aruj and his brother Hayreddin Barbarossa seize Algiers, placing it under Ottoman protection.
Algiers becomes the seat of the Regency of Algiers, a powerful Ottoman-aligned corsair state.
Emperor Charles V's massive expedition to capture Algiers ends in disaster, cementing the Regency's reputation.
The dey of Algiers becomes the de facto ruler, governing the Regency with growing autonomy from Istanbul.
An Anglo-Dutch fleet bombards Algiers to suppress Barbary corsair activity and free Christian captives.
France invades and captures Algiers, beginning a long and brutal colonial conquest of Algeria.
Emir Abdelkader launches a sustained resistance against French occupation, becoming a national hero.
Abdelkader surrenders to French forces, marking a turning point in the conquest of Algeria.
France declares Algeria an integral part of French territory, organized into departments.
The Mokrani Revolt, a major Kabyle uprising, is crushed and followed by harsh land confiscations.
The Code de l'Indigénat institutionalizes legal discrimination against the Muslim Algerian population.
French forces violently suppress the Setif and Guelma uprising, killing thousands of Algerians.
The National Liberation Front (FLN) launches the Algerian War of Independence on 1 November.
The Battle of Algiers sees intense urban guerrilla warfare and French use of torture against the FLN.
The Algerian crisis brings Charles de Gaulle back to power and leads to France's Fifth Republic.
Algeria wins independence on 5 July after a devastating eight-year war, with Ahmed Ben Bella as first leader.
Algeria joins the United Nations as a sovereign state in October.
Houari Boumediene seizes power in a coup, launching state-led industrialization and socialism.
Boumediene nationalizes the oil and gas industry, asserting control over Algeria's hydrocarbon wealth.
A new constitution entrenches single-party FLN rule and socialist principles.
Mass riots over economic hardship force political reforms and the end of the one-party state.
The Islamic Salvation Front (FIS) wins the first round of elections, prompting a military cancellation of the vote.
A military coup voids the elections and bans the FIS, triggering the brutal Algerian Civil War.
Abdelaziz Bouteflika becomes president and promotes a civil concord to end the civil war.
Algerians approve the Charter for Peace and National Reconciliation, largely closing the civil war era.
Arab Spring-era protests prompt the government to lift the long-standing state of emergency.
The Hirak protest movement forces Bouteflika to resign after two decades in power.
Abdelmadjid Tebboune wins re-election as president amid continued economic and political challenges.
